What kinds of requirements do different actors have in introducing smart services?
supported: Answered: Eight requirement categories identified across four actor groups, divided into smart service specific (value, reliability, competence, data security) and general service requirements (attitude, reliance, installed base knowledge, service reputation)

hypothesis: How are the requirements of different actors interconnected when manufacturing firms introduce smart services?
supported: Answered: Four types of interconnectedness mapped (value, reliability, competence, data security); interconnectedness operates as resource interdependence or actors intentions and interpretations

hypothesis: Introducing smart services necessitates fulfilling a combination of multiple actors' interconnected requirements

Value and reliability of smart services are particularly interconnected requirements involving a diversity of actors whose perceptions evolve and change based on relationships and discussions with other actors
General attitudes towards services and the level of knowledge of the installed base of equipment and services affect the perception of the value of smart services
The level of reliance on service employees and their service reputation affects the perception of reliability of smart services and the required competence for offering them

Limited service offering (low SSP focus) + specific investments + customer opportunism + customer integration + high complexity -> servitization success. Symmetrical exchange: customer integration as reciprocal investment under opportunism.

Limited service offering (low SSC focus) + specific investments + customer opportunism + customer integration + high complexity -> servitization success. SSP vs SSC type causally irrelevant when neither is highly focused.

Extensive service offering (high SSP + high SSC) + specific investments + absence of customer opportunism + absence of complexity of customer needs -> servitization success. Low customer-side risk enables one-sided specific investments without need for customer integration.

Extensive service offering (high SSP + high SSC) + absence of specific investments + customer integration + high complexity -> servitization success. Customer-dominant dynamic: customer integration substitutes for specific investments; high coordination costs make specific investments counterproductive with extensive offering.

Extensive service offering (high SSP + high SSC) + absence of customer opportunism + customer integration -> servitization success regardless of demand uncertainty or specific investments. Strong willingness for integration under low opportunism sufficient for advanced servitization success.
Absence of both SSP and SSC focus + absence of specific investments + absence of complexity -> servitization failure. No strategic intent and no demand driver.
Absence of SSP focus + absence of specific investments + absence of customer integration -> servitization failure. Neither provider investments nor customer reciprocal investments present.
Absence of SSC focus + absence of specific investments + customer opportunism -> servitization failure. Absence of specific investments combined with opportunism becomes risk factor for failure.
Absence of SSC focus + absence of specific investments + absence of customer integration -> servitization failure. No provider investment and no customer reciprocal investment.

SSP focus + absence of specific investments + customer opportunism + absence of complexity -> servitization failure. Under stable demand, provider must invest in educating and training customer to avoid failure; opportunism without specific investments impedes success.
